onboarding.welcomeSubtitle: Your personal guide to household finances
onboarding.philosophy: Sthedisia helps you give every euro a purpose. Instead of tracking where money went, we plan ahead where it will go. Simple, no complicated jargon.
onboarding.nameUsage: We'll use this name in the app
onboarding.step1Subtitle: Enter your total net monthly income (salary, freelance, etc.)
onboarding.step2Title: How many people in your household?
onboarding.step2Subtitle: This helps us tailor advice for your household
onboarding.step3Subtitle: Choose one or more goals you'd like to achieve
onboarding.step4Subtitle: Add the accounts you use (bank, cash, cards)
onboarding.step5Subtitle: Start tracking your finances and take control of your budget

householdDesc.one: I'll track only my own finances
householdDesc.two: My partner and I — personal and shared
householdDesc.family: The whole family in one place

onboarding.approxEnough: A rough average is perfectly fine
onboarding.incomeHint: This is only used for smarter tips — you can change it anytime in settings.
onboarding.incomeHintNone: This is only used for smarter tips — you can change it anytime in settings. The app works great even without income information!
onboarding.goalsHint: We tailor tips and recommendations based on your goals. You can change them anytime.
onboarding.accountsHint: Add at least one account to track transactions. Don't worry — the balance doesn't need to be exact, a rough estimate is fine!
onboarding.readyTip: Tip: Start entering transactions right away! The sooner you begin, the more accurate your reports and tips will be.

dashboard.title: Home
dashboard.howAmIDoing: How am I doing this month?
dashboard.statusWarning: Heads up — you're approaching limits in some categories.
dashboard.statusOver: You've exceeded your budget in some categories.
dashboard.noTransactionsHint: Add your first transaction by tapping the "Transactions" tab and start tracking your finances!
dashboard.limitsExplanation: The budget uses the 50/30/20 rule recommended by financial experts (Elizabeth Warren):
dashboard.limitsNeeds: 50% of income — needs (housing 27%, food 13%, transport 5%, utilities 3%, health 2%)
dashboard.limitsWants: 30% of income — wants (entertainment 10%, clothing 5%, personal 5%, education 5%, gifts 5%)
dashboard.limitsSavings: 20% of income — savings and debt repayment (10% each)
dashboard.limitsCustomize: You can customize these limits in the Budget tab → Envelope mode.

transactions.title: Transactions
transactions.sameAccountError: Source and destination account cannot be the same.

budget.title: Budget
budget.setupSubtitle: Give every euro a purpose! Choose a method that works for you.
budget.envelopeExplain: Imagine putting every euro in a separate envelope. Each envelope has its purpose — rent, food, fun... When the envelope is empty, that's it for the month!
budget.rule503020Explain: Simple rule: 50% for needs (rent, bills, food), 30% for wants (entertainment, clothing), 20% for savings and debt.
budget.regenerateConfirm: This will reset all categories to recommended percentages based on the 50/30/20 rule. Continue?
budget.unbudgetedHint: These categories have expenses but no budget set.
budget.noPreviousBudget: No budget from last month to copy.

goals.title: Goals
goals.noGoalsHint: Set your first goal and start saving for something special!
goals.monthlyNeeded: You'll need to save approximately {{amount}} per month for this goal.
goals.deleteConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete the goal "{{name}}"?
goals.netWorthExplain: Net worth = total balance of all accounts minus all debts. The goal is for this number to grow month by month. It's the most important indicator of your financial health.
goals.emergencyFundExplain: It's recommended to have savings covering 3-6 months of your expenses. This is your financial cushion for unexpected situations like job loss, car breakdown, or medical costs.
goals.markCompletedConfirm: Do you want to mark "{{name}}" as completed?

goals.dateInPast: The selected date is in the past. The goal will remain active.
goals.emergencyFundHint: It's recommended to have 3-6 months of expenses as a financial cushion for unexpected situations.

advisor.title: Advisor
advisor.checkinDescription: Take 2 minutes to reflect on your finances this week
advisor.checkinHint: Weekly check-ins help you stay aware of your financial habits and make small adjustments before problems grow.
advisor.learnDescription: Short articles for better understanding of finances
advisor.termsDescription: Financial terms explained in simple language

advice.overspendingMsg: This month you spent {{amount}}€ more than you earned. This is not sustainable long-term. Check which expense categories you can reduce.
advice.lowSavingsMsg: Your savings rate is {{rate}}%. Financial experts recommend at least 20%. Try to find one category where you can cut 10-15%.
advice.greatSavingsMsg: You're saving {{rate}}% of income — above the recommended minimum of 20%. Keep it up! Consider investing the surplus in long-term goals.
advice.overBudgetMsg: You've exceeded your budget in {{count}} categories. It happens — the important thing is to analyse why and adjust next month. An unrealistic budget is worse than no budget.
advice.nearBudgetMsg: You have {{count}} categories at 80%+ of budget. Watch your spending until month-end to stay on track.
advice.highInterestMsg: You have {{count}} debt(s) with an interest rate above 10%. Prioritize paying these off because interest "eats" your savings. Consider the avalanche repayment method.
advice.debtBurdenMsg: Loan payments make up {{ratio}}% of your income. Financial experts recommend below 36%. Consider consolidation or refinancing.
advice.highSubsMsg: Subscriptions cost you {{monthly}}€/mo ({{yearly}}€/yr). Review each subscription and ask yourself: "How often do I actually use this?"
advice.manySubsMsg: You have {{count}} subscriptions. Maybe some can be shared with your partner (family plans) or replaced with free alternatives?
advice.noEmergencyMsg: You don't have enough savings to cover even one month of expenses. This should be priority #1. Start small — even €50 per month is a beginning.
advice.lowEmergencyMsg: Your emergency fund covers {{months}} months of expenses. Good that you've started, but the goal is 3-6 months for real financial peace of mind.
advice.strongEmergencyMsg: You have {{months}} months of expenses in your emergency fund. That's a great position! You can now focus on long-term goals.
advice.negativeNetWorthMsg: Your debts ({{liabilities}}€) exceed your assets ({{assets}}€). This is common while you have loans — focus on reducing debt and building assets.
advice.allGoodTitle: Finances are on the right track!
advice.allGoodMsg: Based on your data, things look good. Keep tracking expenses and sticking to your budget. Consistency is the key to financial health.
advice.errorMsg: Enter more transactions so we can give you personalized advice.
advice.monthlyExpenseUpMsg: You're spending {{percent}}% more than last month. Check which costs increased.
advice.monthlyExpenseDownMsg: You're spending {{percent}}% less than last month. Great job — keep it up!
advice.monthlyIncomeDropMsg: Your income decreased by {{percent}}% compared to last month. Consider adjusting your budget.
advice.monthlySavingsImprovedMsg: Your savings rate increased from {{previous}}% to {{current}}%. Great progress!

education.budgetTitle: What is a budget and why do you need one?
education.budgetSummary: A budget is not a restriction — it's a plan that gives you freedom.
education.budgetContent:
A budget sounds scary, but it's actually simple: it's a plan for where your money goes.

Imagine your income is a pie. A budget is how you slice that pie — how much for rent, food, entertainment, savings.

WITHOUT a budget money "disappears" and at the end of the month you wonder where it went. With a budget, YOU decide.

The popular 50/30/20 method:
• 50% for needs (rent, bills, food)
• 30% for wants (outings, hobbies, shopping)
• 20% for savings and debt repayment

You don't have to be perfect. The goal is awareness — knowing where money goes. That alone changes behavior.
education.emergencySummary: Why you need 3-6 months of expenses set aside.
education.emergencyContent:
Imagine your car breaks down, you lose your job, or you need urgent medical care. Without an emergency fund, that becomes a crisis. With one — it's just an inconvenience.

How much do you need? 3-6 months of basic expenses (rent, food, bills, loan payments).

If your monthly expenses are €1,500, you need €4,500-9,000 set aside.

How to get there?
1. Start small — even €50/month is €600/year
2. Open a separate savings account (so you don't "see" it)
3. Automate the transfer right when your paycheck arrives
4. Every bonus, tax refund or gift — part goes into the fund

Don't give up if the goal seems far. Every euro makes you more secure.
education.compoundTitle: Compound Interest: The 8th Wonder of the World
education.compoundSummary: How your money earns money, which earns money...
education.compoundContent:
Einstein allegedly said compound interest is "the most powerful force in the universe". What does that mean?

Simple interest: €1,000 × 5% = €50/year. Every year, €50.

Compound interest: €1,000 × 5% = €50 in year one. But in year two, 5% is calculated on €1,050, giving you €52.50. And so on — interest on interest.

Over 30 years:
• €1,000 with simple interest → €2,500
• €1,000 with compound interest → €4,322

But beware — this works AGAINST you on debts too! Loan interest also "compounds".

Rule of 72: divide 72 by the interest rate to get the number of years to double your money.
72 ÷ 7% = ~10 years to double.

The message: start early, be patient, and let compound interest work for you.
education.debtTitle: How to Get Rid of Debt: Snowball vs Avalanche
education.debtSummary: Two proven strategies for debt repayment.
education.debtContent:
If you have multiple debts, two strategies are most popular:

⛄ SNOWBALL:
You pay off the SMALLEST debt first, while paying the minimum on others.
+ Quick wins keep you motivated!
- You pay more interest overall.

🏔️ AVALANCHE:
You pay off the debt with the HIGHEST interest first.
+ Mathematically optimal — you pay less interest.
- Can be demotivating as larger debts shrink slowly.

Which is better? Depends on you:
• If you need motivation → Snowball
• If you want to minimize costs → Avalanche
• Both work! The most important thing is to pay SOMETHING above the minimum.

Tip: take any "extra" money (bonus, tax return, reduced expense) and put it on a debt. Even €50 extra per month can shorten repayment by years.
education.lifestyleTitle: Lifestyle Inflation: The Hidden Savings Killer
education.lifestyleSummary: Why do you earn more but never have enough?
education.lifestyleContent:
Got a €300 raise? Great! But suddenly you have a better phone, a pricier restaurant, a new hobby... and still no surplus.

That's "lifestyle inflation" — as you earn more, you spend more. Result: you never save more.

How to fight it:
1. "Pay yourself first" — when you get a raise, automatically increase savings by half the amount
2. Wait 30 days before big purchases
3. Track expenses — awareness is half the battle
4. Ask yourself: "Is this something I truly want, or does it just feel like I can afford it now?"

The point isn't to live frugally. The point is to spend on what TRULY brings you happiness, not what "comes with a higher income".

Remember: wealth isn't how much you earn — it's how much you KEEP.

glossary.budgetDef: A plan that determines how much money goes where. Like a diet, but for money.
glossary.netWorthDef: Everything you own (assets) minus everything you owe. Your financial "score".
glossary.savingsRateDef: The percentage of income you save. Goal: at least 20%.
glossary.emergencyFundDef: Money set aside for a rainy day. Recommended: 3-6 months of expenses.
glossary.compoundInterestDef: Interest on interest — your money earns money, which earns money...
glossary.liquidityDef: How quickly you can convert something to cash. Savings account = high liquidity, real estate = low.
glossary.inflationDef: Rising prices over time. €100 today buys less than €100 ten years ago.
glossary.amortizationDef: The loan repayment schedule — how much goes to interest vs. principal.
glossary.diversificationDef: "Don't put all your eggs in one basket." Spread investments across multiple places.
glossary.passiveIncomeDef: Money you earn without active work — rent, dividends, interest.
glossary.cashFlowDef: The difference between money coming in and going out. Positive = great, negative = problem.
glossary.roiDef: Return on Investment — how much you earned relative to what you invested. "Was it worth it?"

personalizedTips.overspending: This month you're spending more than you earn. Let's look together for where we can cut.
personalizedTips.lowSavingsRate: Your savings rate is {{rate}}%. The goal is at least 20% — let's try to find room for improvement.

personalizedTips.overBudget: You've exceeded the budget for: {{categories}}. Maybe it's time for more realistic limits?

household.title: Household
household.noHousehold: You're not part of a household

household.joinError: Invite code not found. Check the code and try again.
household.inviteCodeHint: Share this code with your partner so they can join your household.
household.leaveConfirmMessage: Your data stays, but you won't see other members' balances anymore.
household.syncDescription: Export your data as a JSON file and share it with your partner via Google Drive, AirDrop, or other methods.
household.importConfirmMessage: This will add your partner's data to your household. Your existing data won't be deleted.
household.importError: Invalid file format or import error.
household.syncHint: Tip: Create a shared folder on Google Drive and save exported files there. That way you both have access to the latest data.

list: Before buying something, ask yourself: 'Do I need it or want it?' This simple distinction can save hundreds of euros monthly.
Try the '24-hour rule' — when you see something expensive, wait a day. You'll often realize you don't actually need it.
Reviewing subscriptions monthly can reveal services you don't use. The average person spends €40-60 on forgotten subscriptions!
Even €50 per month in savings is €600 per year. Small steps create big results.
Cooking at home instead of ordering delivery can save €200-400 monthly for a two-person household.
Automated savings work wonders — set up automatic transfers to savings right after payday.
Compare prices at two or three stores before bigger purchases. Online comparison tools save both time and money.
An emergency fund covering 3 months of expenses is your financial cushion. Start small and build gradually.
Make a list before going to the store. Impulse purchases account for about 20% of total food spending.
Annual subscriptions are usually 20-40% cheaper than monthly ones. Check if you can switch some.
When you get a raise, 'pay yourself first' — put at least half the raise into savings before getting used to higher spending.
Tap water instead of bottled can save €200-500 per year. Plus, it's better for the environment!
Set goals you're excited about. Saving for a vacation is more motivating than 'saving just in case'.
Track expenses for at least 3 months to see real spending patterns. We're often surprised by which categories cost the most.
Share expenses with your partner transparently. A shared budget reduces stress and money conflicts.
LED bulbs use 75% less electricity. Replacing all bulbs can save €50-100 per year.
Plan meals on Sunday for the whole week. You'll waste less food and order less delivery.
Before a big purchase, ask: 'How many hours do I need to work to earn this?' Puts the price in perspective.
Cashback cards can return 1-3% of spending. On an annual basis, that could be €100-300.
Don't compare yourself to others on social media. Most people only show the nice side of finances.
A 50% discount on something you don't need isn't savings — it's an expense. Only buy what you'd buy at full price too.
Energy rating of appliances matters. An A+++ fridge uses half the electricity of an old model.
Once a month, review all automatic charges. You'd be surprised how many services you forget to cancel.
A shared budget doesn't mean equal amounts — it means proportional to income. It's fair when everyone contributes according to their means.
Investing in education (courses, books, conferences) has the highest return — in yourself.

notifications.weeklyBody: Check how you're doing with your budget this week. A little control = big savings!
notifications.monthlyBody: New month, new budget! Review last month and set goals for this one.
notifications.testBody: Notifications are enabled! You'll receive payment reminders and financial tips.
